title: Online latent tyre-state features from a resettable state-space filter
summary: A recent F1 tire-degradation paper models latent pace/degradation with explicit pit-stop resets and rolling-origin evaluation, arguing that observed lap times are noisy proxies for an underlying degradation state.
rationale: A recent F1 tire-degradation paper models latent pace/degradation with explicit pit-stop resets and rolling-origin evaluation, arguing that observed lap times are noisy proxies for an underlying degradation state. That is a tighter version of 'latent tyre state' than the current hand-built ratios and could expose hidden wear, warm-up, and uncertainty signals.
implementation_hint: Fit a lightweight per-driver-race online filter using only past laps to estimate latent pace level, degradation slope, post-pit reset level, and state uncertainty. Feed those filtered states as new numeric features into the existing GBDT rather than replacing the whole model.
expected_effect: Should help separate genuinely worn tyres from noisy slow laps, improving discrimination near the pit window and reducing overreaction to one-off traffic/errors.
risk: If the filter is misspecified or too expensive, it may add noise; wet/mixed races and abnormal out-laps may need separate handling.
